evidence_dialogues.utterance: Call it what you like, we could not do otherwise.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: And you should not have let your name appear in the affair. There was no need for anyone to know that the attack on the Baths came from you. You ought to have consulted me, Dr. Stockmann.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Perhaps Mr. Hovstad had better—

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Well, the fact is that, now we know the bearings of the whole affair, we think we might venture to put the "People's Messenger" at your disposal.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: We should prefer to help you without any return whatever, believe me. But the "People's Messenger" is in rather a shaky condition...

reasoning_steps.inference: Hovstad begins by defending their betrayal the previous night, stating they couldn't do otherwise.
Once they mention Kiil buying shares, Hovstad criticizes Dr. Stockmann's lack of finesse in hiding his involvement in the 'scheme'.
Finally, having assumed it is a profitable scheme, Hovstad (with Aslaksen) offers the paper's support while heavily implying he needs financial rescue in return.

qa.question: What specifically did Hovstad threaten to do if Dr. Stockmann refused to financially rescue his newspaper?
option: A. He threatened to burn down the Stockmann house.
B. He threatened to go to the police and have Dr. Stockmann arrested for assault.
C. He threatened to take an evil view of the affair and relentlessly hunt the doctor down in the press.
D. He threatened to tell Mr. Kiil that the doctor was planning to steal the inheritance.
E. He threatened to permanently shut down the newspaper and blame the doctor for the job losses.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: And suppose I don't give you a single penny of all I get out of it? Money is not very easy to get out of us rich folk, please to remember!

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: And you please to remember that this affair of the shares can be represented in two ways!

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Yes, and you are just the man to do it. If I don't come to the rescue of the "People's Messenger," you will certainly take an evil view of the affair; you will hunt me down, I can well imagine—pursue me—try to throttle me as a dog does a hare.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: It is a natural law; every animal must fight for its own livelihood.

reasoning_steps.inference: Dr. Stockmann asks what happens if he gives no money, and Hovstad warns that the affair can be represented in two ways in the press.
Dr. Stockmann outlines the threat (taking an evil view, hunting him down), and Hovstad confirms this vicious intent by citing 'natural law'.

qa.question: What is the professional relationship between Hovstad and Aslaksen?
option: A. They are fierce competitors running rival newspapers in the town.
B. They are collaborators and representatives of the 'People's Messenger' newspaper.
C. Hovstad is the mayor, and Aslaksen is his appointed chief of police.
D. Hovstad is the landlord of the building where Aslaksen operates his printing press.
E. They are a doctor and patient, meeting by chance at the Stockmann house.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Perhaps Mr. Hovstad had better—

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: No, you tell him, Aslaksen.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Well, the fact is that, now we know the bearings of the whole affair, we think we might venture to put the "People's Messenger" at your disposal.

reasoning_steps.inference: Hovstad and Aslaksen arrive together and coordinate who should speak.
They act as a unified front offering the 'People's Messenger' to the doctor, showing they are collaborative partners in running/managing the paper.

qa.question: How many individuals did Dr. Stockmann explicitly order out of the window?
option: A. One
B. Two
C. Three
D. Four
E. Five

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Out of the window with you, Mr. Hovstad!

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Out of the window, Mr. Aslaksen! Jump, I tell you! You will have to do it, sooner or later.

reasoning_steps.inference: Dr. Stockmann specifically targets Mr. Hovstad to go out the window.
Dr. Stockmann then specifically targets a second person, Mr. Aslaksen, to jump out the window, making the total two.

qa.question: Which statement best defines Morten Kiil's primary motivation for his actions regarding the Baths?
option: A. He is deeply concerned about public health and wants to ensure the town's water is thoroughly cleaned.
B. He wants to help his son-in-law become the most powerful political figure in the entire country.
C. He is fiercely protective of his own reputation and wants to clear his name from accusations of pollution.
D. He is a greedy capitalist who only bought the shares to sell them at a massive profit the next day.
E. He feels guilty for polluting the water and wants to donate his entire fortune to the poor to make amends.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Morten Kiil
evidence_dialogues.utterance: You said yesterday that the worst of this pollution came from my tannery. If that is true, then my grandfather and my father before me, and I myself, for many years past, have been poisoning the town like three destroying angels. Do you think I am going to sit quiet under that reproach?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Morten Kiil
evidence_dialogues.utterance: No, thank you. I am jealous of my name and reputation. They call me "the Badger," I am told. A badger is a kind of pig, I believe; but I am not going to give them the right to call me that. I mean to live and die a clean man.

reasoning_steps.inference: Kiil expresses outrage that Dr. Stockmann blamed his family's tannery for the pollution.
He explicitly states he is jealous of his name and reputation and refuses to die with the stigma of being a polluter, which drives his blackmail attempt.

qa.question: What was Morten Kiil's immediate emotional tone when he first confronted Dr. Stockmann about the condition of his house?
option: A. He was profoundly sad and offered sympathy for the broken windows.
B. He was terrified of the mob and begged Dr. Stockmann to protect him.
C. He was highly amused and sarcastic, mocking the doctor's 'comfortable' situation.
D. He was apologetic and confessed he had accidentally caused the riot.
E. He was deeply confused and repeatedly asked who had damaged the property.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Morten Kiil
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I won't sit. You look very comfortable here today, Thomas.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Yes, don't we!

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Morten Kiil
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Very comfortable—plenty of fresh air. I should think you have got enough to-day of that oxygen you were talking about yesterday. Your conscience must be in splendid order to-day, I should think.

reasoning_steps.inference: Despite the room having broken windows and being in disorder, Kiil calls it 'very comfortable'.
He continues his sarcasm by calling the draft from the broken panes 'plenty of fresh air' and mocking the doctor's previous talks about oxygen and conscience.

qa.question: What sequence of actions defines Morten Kiil's blackmail attempt against Dr. Stockmann?
option: A. He first threatened Katherine, then bought the shares, and finally demanded an apology to the mayor.
B. He first bought all the cheap Baths shares using Katherine's inheritance, then demanded the doctor clear his tannery's name, and finally gave him a strict deadline.
C. He first gave a strict deadline, then asked for a scientific antidote, and finally bought the shares.
D. He first offered the shares as a gift, then withdrew the offer when insulted, and finally donated them to charity.
E. He first brought Hovstad as a witness, then demanded the doctor buy the shares from him, and finally left in anger.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Morten Kiil
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Yes, the whole of the money is invested in the Baths now. And now I just want to see whether you are quite stark, staring mad, Thomas!

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Morten Kiil
evidence_dialogues.utterance: You shall cleanse me, Thomas.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Morten Kiil
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Then I had better go. But I must have an answer from you before two o'clock—yes or no. If it is no, the shares go to a charity, and that this very day.

reasoning_steps.inference: Kiil begins by revealing he has already bought the shares with the money meant for Katherine and the children.
He then makes his demand: the doctor must publicly 'cleanse' his reputation regarding the tannery.
Finally, he sets a strict temporal deadline, giving the doctor until two o'clock to decide before the shares are given away.

qa.question: What specifically did Morten Kiil threaten to do if Dr. Stockmann refused his terms?
option: A. He threatened to have Dr. Stockmann thrown into prison for defamation.
B. He threatened to immediately donate the shares (and thus the family's inheritance) to charity.
C. He threatened to physically assault the doctor with his own umbrella.
D. He threatened to burn down the remaining structures of the Baths.
E. He threatened to permanently disown Katherine and never speak to his grandchildren again.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Morten Kiil
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Then I had better go. But I must have an answer from you before two o'clock—yes or no. If it is no, the shares go to a charity, and that this very day.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: And what does Katherine get?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Morten Kiil
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Not a halfpenny. Look at those two!

reasoning_steps.inference: Kiil gives an ultimatum with a specific consequence if the answer is no.
He explicitly states that if refused, the shares will go to a charity that very day, leaving Katherine with 'not a halfpenny'.

qa.question: What is the relationship between Morten Kiil and Dr. Stockmann?
option: A. Morten Kiil is Dr. Stockmann's landlord who is evicting him from his current house.
B. Morten Kiil is Dr. Stockmann's father-in-law, whose money is intended for the doctor's wife.
C. Morten Kiil is Dr. Stockmann's medical patient who suffers from delusions.
D. Morten Kiil is Dr. Stockmann's younger brother who runs a rival medical practice.
E. Morten Kiil is Dr. Stockmann's former schoolteacher who taught him science.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Petra
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Father, grandfather is here, asking if he may speak to you alone.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Certainly he may. Come in, Mr. Kiil. What can I do for you? Won't you sit down?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: And you have gone and taken Katherine's money for this!

reasoning_steps.inference: Petra refers to Morten Kiil as her grandfather when speaking to her father (Dr. Stockmann).
This makes Morten Kiil the father of Dr. Stockmann's wife, Katherine, confirming he is Dr. Stockmann's father-in-law.

qa.question: By what precise hour did Morten Kiil demand an answer from Dr. Stockmann?
option: A. Twelve o'clock
B. One o'clock
C. Two o'clock
D. Three o'clock
E. Four o'clock

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Morten Kiil
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Then I had better go. But I must have an answer from you before two o'clock—yes or no. If it is no, the shares go to a charity, and that this very day.

reasoning_steps.inference: Morten Kiil sets a strict numerical deadline for when he must receive his answer.

session_5.speaker: NARRATION
session_5.text: (SCENE.—DR. STOCKMANN'S study. Bookcases and cabinets containing specimens, line the walls. At the back is a door leading to the hall; in the foreground on the left, a door leading to the sitting-room. In the righthand wall are two windows, of which all the panes are broken. The DOCTOR'S desk, littered with books and papers, stands in the middle of the room, which is in disorder. It is morning. DR. STOCKMANN in dressing-gown, slippers and a smoking-cap, is bending down and raking with an umbrella under one of the cabinets. After a little while he rakes out a stone.)

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Katherine, I have found another one.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Oh, you will find a lot more yet, I expect.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: I shall treasure these stones as relics. Ejlif and Morten shall look at them every day, and when they are grown up they shall inherit them as heirlooms. Hasn't—what the deuce is her name?—the girl, you know—hasn't she been to fetch the glazier yet?

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, but he said he didn't know if he would be able to come today.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: You will see he won't dare to come.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Well, that is just what Randine thought—that he didn't dare to, on account of the neighbours. What is it you want, Randine? Give it to me. Here is a letter for you, Thomas.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Let me see it. Ah!—of course.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Who is it from?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: From the landlord. Notice to quit.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Is it possible? Such a nice man

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Does not dare do otherwise, he says. Doesn't like doing it, but dare not do otherwise—on account of his fellow-citizens—out of regard for public opinion. Is in a dependent position—dares not offend certain influential men.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: There, you see, Thomas!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, yes, I see well enough; the whole lot of them in the town are cowards; not a man among them dares do anything for fear of the others. But it doesn't matter to us, Katherine. We are going to sail away to the New World,

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: But, Thomas, are you sure we are well advised to take this step?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Are you suggesting that I should stay here, where they have pilloried me as an enemy of the people—branded me—broken my windows! And just look here, Katherine—they have torn a great rent in my black trousers too!

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Oh, dear!—and they are the best pair you have got!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: You should never wear your best trousers when you go out to fight for freedom and truth. It is not that I care so much about the trousers, you know; you can always sew them up again for me. But that the common herd should dare to make this attack on me, as if they were my equals—that is what I cannot, for the life of me, swallow!

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: There is no doubt they have behaved very ill toward you, Thomas; but is that sufficient reason for our leaving our native country for good and all?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: If we went to another town, do you suppose we should not find the common people just as insolent as they are here? Depend upon it, there is not much to choose between them. Oh, well, let the curs snap—that is not the worst part of it. The worst is that, from one end of this country to the other, every man is the slave of his Party. Although, as far as that goes, I daresay it is not much better in the free West either; the compact majority, and liberal public opinion, and all that infernal old bag of tricks are probably rampant there too. But there things are done on a larger scale, you see. They may kill you, but they won't put you to death by slow torture. They don't squeeze a free man's soul in a vice, as they do here. And, if need be, one can live in solitude. (Walks up and down.) If only I knew where there was a virgin forest or a small South Sea island for sale, cheap—

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: But think of the boys, Thomas!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: What a strange woman you are, Katherine! Would you prefer to have the boys grow up in a society like this? You saw for yourself last night that half the population are out of their minds; and if the other half have not lost their senses, it is because they are mere brutes, with no sense to lose.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: But, Thomas dear, the imprudent things you said had something to do with it, you know.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Well, isn't what I said perfectly true? Don't they turn every idea topsy-turvy? Don't they make a regular hotchpotch of right and wrong? Don't they say that the things I know are true, are lies? The craziest part of it all is the fact of these "liberals," men of full age, going about in crowds imagining that they are the broad-minded party! Did you ever hear anything like it, Katherine!

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, yes, it's mad enough of them, certainly; but—

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Yes. I have been given notice of dismissal.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Dismissal?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: You too?

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Mrs. Busk gave me my notice; so I thought it was best to go at once.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: You were perfectly right, too!

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Who would have thought Mrs. Busk was a woman like that!

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Mrs. Busk isn't a bit like that, mother; I saw quite plainly how it hurt her to do it. But she didn't dare do otherwise, she said; and so I got my notice.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: She didn't dare do otherwise, either! It's delicious!

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Well, after the dreadful scenes last night—

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: It was not only that. Just listen to this, father!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Well?

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Mrs. Busk showed me no less than three letters she received this morning—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Anonymous, I suppose?

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Yes.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, because they didn't dare to risk signing their names, Katherine!

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: And two of them were to the effect that a man, who has been our guest here, was declaring last night at the Club that my views on various subjects are extremely emancipated—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: You did not deny that, I hope?

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: No, you know I wouldn't. Mrs. Busk's own views are tolerably emancipated, when we are alone together; but now that this report about me is being spread, she dare not keep me on any longer.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: And someone who had been a guest of ours! That shows you the return you get for your hospitality, Thomas!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: We won't live in such a disgusting hole any longer. Pack up as quickly as you can, Katherine; the sooner we can get away, the better.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Be quiet—I think I hear someone in the hall. See who it is, Petra.

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Oh, it's you, Captain Horster! Do come in.

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: Good morning. I thought I would just come in and see how you were.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Thanks—that is really kind of you.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: And thank you, too, for helping us through the crowd,

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: How did you manage to get home again?

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: Oh, somehow or other. I am fairly strong, and there is more sound than fury about these folk.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, isn't their swinish cowardice astonishing? Look here, I will show you something! There are all the stones they have thrown through my windows. Just look at them! I'm hanged if there are more than two decently large bits of hard stone in the whole heap; the rest are nothing but gravel—wretched little things. And yet they stood out there bawling and swearing that they would do me some violence; but as for doing anything—you don't see much of that in this town.

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: Just as well for you this time, doctor!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: True enough. But it makes one angry all the same; because if some day it should be a question of a national fight in real earnest, you will see that public opinion will be in favour of taking to one's heels, and the compact majority will turn tail like a flock of sheep, Captain Horster. That is what is so mournful to think of; it gives me so much concern, that—. No, devil take it, it is ridiculous to care about it! They have called me an enemy of the people, so an enemy of the people let me be!

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: You will never be that, Thomas.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Don't swear to that, Katherine. To be called an ugly name may have the same effect as a pin-scratch in the lung. And that hateful name—I can't get quit of it. It is sticking here in the pit of my stomach, eating into me like a corrosive acid. And no magnesia will remove it.

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Bah!—you should only laugh at them, father,

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: They will change their minds some day, Doctor.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, Thomas, as sure as you are standing here.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Perhaps, when it is too late. Much good may it do them! They may wallow in their filth then and rue the day when they drove a patriot into exile. When do you sail, Captain Horster?

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: Hm!—that was just what I had come to speak about—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Why, has anything gone wrong with the ship?

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: No; but what has happened is that I am not to sail in it.

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Do you mean that you have been dismissed from your command?

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: Yes, that's just it.

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: You too.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: There, you see, Thomas!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: And that for the truth's sake! Oh, if I had thought such a thing possible—

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: You mustn't take it to heart; I shall be sure to find a job with some ship-owner or other, elsewhere.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: And that is this man Vik—a wealthy man, independent of everyone and everything—! Shame on him!

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: He is quite an excellent fellow otherwise; he told me himself he would willingly have kept me on, if only he had dared—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: But he didn't dare? No, of course not.

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: It is not such an easy matter, he said, for a party man—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: The worthy man spoke the truth. A party is like a sausage machine; it mashes up all sorts of heads together into the same mincemeat—fatheads and blockheads, all in one mash!

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Come, come, Thomas dear!

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: If only you had not come home with us, things might not have come to this pass.

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: I do not regret it.

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Thank you for that!

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: And so what I came to say was that if you are determined to go away, I have thought of another plan—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: That's splendid!—if only we can get away at once.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Hush!—wasn't that some one knocking?

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: That is uncle, surely.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Aha! Come in!

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Dear Thomas, promise me definitely—

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: Oh, you are engaged. In that case, I will—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: No, no, come in.

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: But I wanted to speak to you alone.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: We will go into the sitting-room in the meanwhile.

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: And I will look in again later.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: No, go in there with them, Captain Horster; I want to hear more about—.

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: Very well, I will wait, then.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: I daresay you find it rather draughty here today. Put your hat on.

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: Thank you, if I may. I think I caught cold last night; I stood and shivered—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Really? I found it warm enough.

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: I regret that it was not in my power to prevent those excesses last night.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Have you anything in particular to say to me besides that?

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: I have this document for you, from the Baths Committee.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: My dismissal?

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, dating from today. It gives us pain to do it; but, to speak frankly, we dared not do otherwise on account of public opinion.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Dared not? I seem to have heard that word before, today.

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: I must beg you to understand your position clearly. For the future you must not count on any practice whatever in the town.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Devil take the practice! But why are you so sure of that?

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: The Householders' Association is circulating a list from house to house. All right-minded citizens are being called upon to give up employing you; and I can assure you that not a single head of a family will risk refusing his signature. They simply dare not.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: No, no; I don't doubt it. But what then?

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: If I might advise you, it would be best to leave the place for a little while—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, the propriety of leaving the place has occurred to me.

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: Good. And then, when you have had six months to think things over, if, after mature consideration, you can persuade yourself to write a few words of regret, acknowledging your error—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: I might have my appointment restored to me, do you mean?

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: Perhaps. It is not at all impossible.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: But what about public opinion, then? Surely you would not dare to do it on account of public feeling...

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: Public opinion is an extremely mutable thing. And, to be quite candid with you, it is a matter of great importance to us to have some admission of that sort from you in writing.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Oh, that's what you are after, is it! I will just trouble you to remember what I said to you lately about foxy tricks of that sort!

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: Your position was quite different then. At that time you had reason to suppose you had the whole town at your back—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, and now I feel I have the whole town ON my back— I would not do it if I had the devil and his dam on my back—! Never—never, I tell you!

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: A man with a family has no right to behave as you do.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: I have no right! There is only one single thing in the world a free man has no right to do. Do you know what that is?

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: No

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Of course you don't, but I will tell you. A free man has no right to soil himself with filth; he has no right to behave in a way that would justify his spitting in his own face.

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: This sort of thing sounds extremely plausible, of course; and if there were no other explanation for your obstinacy—. But as it happens that there is.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: What do you mean?

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: You understand, very well what I mean. But, as your brother and as a man of discretion, I advise you not to build too much upon expectations and prospects that may so very easily fail you.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: What in the world is all this about?

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: Do you really ask me to believe that you are ignorant of the terms of Mr. Kiil's will?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: I know that the small amount he possesses is to go to an institution for indigent old workpeople. How does that concern me?

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: In the first place, it is by no means a small amount that is in question. Mr. Kiil is a fairly wealthy man.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: I had no notion of that!

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: Hm!—hadn't you really? Then I suppose you had no notion, either, that a considerable portion of his wealth will come to your children, you and your wife having a life-rent of the capital. Has he never told you so?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Never, on my honour! Quite the reverse; he has consistently done nothing but fume at being so unconscionably heavily taxed. But are you perfectly certain of this, Peter?

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: I have it from an absolutely reliable source.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Then, thank God, Katherine is provided for—and the children too! I must tell her this at once—Katherine, Katherine!

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: Hush, don't say a word yet!

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: What is the matter?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Oh, nothing, nothing; you can go back. Provided for!—Just think of it, we are all provided for! And for life! What a blessed feeling it is to know one is provided for!

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, but that is just exactly what you are not. Mr. Kiil can alter his will any day he likes.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: But he won't do that, my dear Peter. The "Badger" is much too delighted at my attack on you and your wise friends.

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: Ah, that throws a light on various things.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: What things?

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: I see that the whole thing was a combined manoeuvre on your part and his. These violent, reckless attacks that you have made against the leading men of the town, under the pretence that it was in the name of truth—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: What about them?

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: I see that they were nothing else than the stipulated price for that vindictive old man's will.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Peter—you are the most disgusting plebeian I have ever met in all my life.

session_5.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_5.text: All is over between us. Your dismissal is irrevocable—we have a weapon against you now.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: For shame! For shame! Katherine, you must have the floor scrubbed after him! Let—what's her name—devil take it, the girl who has always got soot on her nose—

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Hush, Thomas, be quiet!

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Father, grandfather is here, asking if he may speak to you alone.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Certainly he may. Come in, Mr. Kiil. What can I do for you? Won't you sit down?

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: I won't sit. You look very comfortable here today, Thomas.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, don't we!

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: Very comfortable—plenty of fresh air. I should think you have got enough to-day of that oxygen you were talking about yesterday. Your conscience must be in splendid order to-day, I should think.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: It is.

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: So I should think. Do you know what I have got here?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: A good conscience, too, I hope.

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: Bah!—No, it is something better than that.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Shares in the Baths?

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: They were not difficult to get today.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: And you have been buying—?

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: As many as I could pay for.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: But, my dear Mr. Kiil—consider the state of the Baths' affairs!

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: If you behave like a reasonable man, you can soon set the Baths on their feet again.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Well, you can see for yourself that I have done all I can, but—. They are all mad in this town!

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: You said yesterday that the worst of this pollution came from my tannery. If that is true, then my grandfather and my father before me, and I myself, for many years past, have been poisoning the town like three destroying angels. Do you think I am going to sit quiet under that reproach?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Unfortunately I am afraid you will have to.

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: No, thank you. I am jealous of my name and reputation. They call me "the Badger," I am told. A badger is a kind of pig, I believe; but I am not going to give them the right to call me that. I mean to live and die a clean man.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: And how are you going to set about it?

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: You shall cleanse me, Thomas.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: I!

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: Do you know what money I have bought these shares with?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: And you have gone and taken Katherine's money for this!

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: Yes, the whole of the money is invested in the Baths now. And now I just want to see whether you are quite stark, staring mad, Thomas! If you still make out that these animals and other nasty things of that sort come from my tannery, it will be exactly as if you were to flay broad strips of skin from Katherine's body, and Petra's, and the boys'; and no decent man would do that—unless he were mad.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, but I am mad; I am mad!

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: You cannot be so absurdly mad as all that, when it is a question of your wife and children.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Why couldn't you consult me about it, before you went and bought all that trash?

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: What is done cannot be undone.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: If only I were not so certain about it—! But I am absolutely convinced that I am right.

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: If you stick to your mad idea, this won't be worth much, you know.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: But, hang it all! It might be possible for science to discover some prophylactic, I should think—or some antidote of some kind—

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: To kill these animals, do you mean?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, or to make them innocuous.

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: Couldn't you try some rat's-bane?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Don't talk nonsense! They all say it is only imagination, you know. Well, let it go at that! Let them have their own way about it! Haven't the ignorant, narrow-minded curs reviled me as an enemy of the people?—and haven't they been ready to tear the clothes off my back too?

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: And broken all your windows to pieces!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: And then there is my duty to my family. I must talk it over with Katherine; she is great on those things.

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: That is right; be guided by a reasonable woman's advice.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: To think you could do such a preposterous thing!  Risking Katherine's money in this way, and putting me in such a horribly painful dilemma! When I look at you, I think I see the devil himself—.

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: Then I had better go. But I must have an answer from you before two o'clock—yes or no. If it is no, the shares go to a charity, and that this very day.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: And what does Katherine get?

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: Not a halfpenny. Look at those two!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: What the devil!—have YOU actually the face to come into my house?

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: Certainly.

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: We have something to say to you, you see.

session_5.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_5.text: Yes or no—before two o'clock.

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: Aha!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Well, what do you want with me? Be brief.

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: I can quite understand that you are annoyed with us for our attitude at the meeting yesterday.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Attitude, do you call it? Yes, it was a charming attitude! I call it weak, womanish—damnably shameful!

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: Call it what you like, we could not do otherwise.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: You DARED not do otherwise—isn't that it?

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: Well, if you like to put it that way.

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: But why did you not let us have word of it beforehand?—just a hint to Mr. Hovstad or to me?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: A hint? Of what?

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: Of what was behind it all.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: I don't understand you in the least—

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: Oh yes, you do, Dr. Stockmann.

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: It is no good making a mystery of it any longer.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: What the devil do you both mean?

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: May I ask if your father-in-law is not going round the town buying up all the shares in the Baths?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, he has been buying Baths shares today; but—

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: It would have been more prudent to get someone else to do it—someone less nearly related to you.

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: And you should not have let your name appear in the affair. There was no need for anyone to know that the attack on the Baths came from you. You ought to have consulted me, Dr. Stockmann.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Are such things conceivable? Are such things possible?

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: Evidently they are. But it is better to use a little finesse, you know.

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: And it is much better to have several persons in a thing of that sort; because the responsibility of each individual is lessened, when there are others with him.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Come to the point, gentlemen. What do you want?

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: Perhaps Mr. Hovstad had better—

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: No, you tell him, Aslaksen.

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: Well, the fact is that, now we know the bearings of the whole affair, we think we might venture to put the "People's Messenger" at your disposal.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Do you dare do that now? What about public opinion? Are you not afraid of a storm breaking upon our heads?

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: We will try to weather it.

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: And you must be ready to go off quickly on a new tack, Doctor. As soon as your invective has done its work—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Do you mean, as soon as my father-in-law and I have got hold of the shares at a low figure?

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: Your reasons for wishing to get the control of the Baths are mainly scientific, I take it.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Of course; it was for scientific reasons that I persuaded the old "Badger" to stand in with me in the matter. So we will tinker at the conduit-pipes a little, and dig up a little bit of the shore, and it shan't cost the town a sixpence. That will be all right—eh?

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: I think so—if you have the "People's Messenger" behind you.

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: The Press is a power in a free community. Doctor.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Quite so. And so is public opinion. And you, Mr. Aslaksen—I suppose you will be answerable for the Householders' Association?

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: Yes, and for the Temperance Society. You may rely on that.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: But, gentlemen—I really am ashamed to ask the question—but, what return do you—?

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: We should prefer to help you without any return whatever, believe me. But the "People's Messenger" is in rather a shaky condition; it doesn't go really well; and I should be very unwilling to suspend the paper now, when there is so much work to do here in the political way.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Quite so; that would be a great trial to such a friend of the people as you are. But I am an enemy of the people, remember! Where have I put my stick? Where the devil is my stick?

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: What's that?

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: Surely you never mean—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: And suppose I don't give you a single penny of all I get out of it? Money is not very easy to get out of us rich folk, please to remember!

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: And you please to remember that this affair of the shares can be represented in two ways!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, and you are just the man to do it. If I don't come to the rescue of the "People's Messenger," you will certainly take an evil view of the affair; you will hunt me down, I can well imagine—pursue me—try to throttle me as a dog does a hare.

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: It is a natural law; every animal must fight for its own livelihood.

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: And get its food where it can, you know.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Then you go and look for yours in the gutter; because I am going to show you which is the strongest animal of us three! Ah, now—!

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: You are surely not going to use violence!

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: Take care what you are doing with that umbrella.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Out of the window with you, Mr. Hovstad!

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: Are you quite mad!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Out of the window, Mr. Aslaksen! Jump, I tell you! You will have to do it, sooner or later.

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: Moderation, Doctor—I am a delicate man—I can stand so little—(calls out) help, help!

session_5.speaker: STAGE
session_5.text: (MRS. STOCKMANN, PETRA and HORSTER come in from the sitting-room.)

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Good gracious, Thomas! What is happening?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Jump out, I tell you! Out into the gutter!

session_5.speaker: Hovstad
session_5.text: An assault on an unoffending man! I call you to witness, Captain Horster.

session_5.speaker: Aslaksen
session_5.text: If only I knew the way about here—.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Control yourself, Thomas!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Upon my soul, they have escaped after all.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: What did they want you to do?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: I will tell you later on; I have something else to think about now. Look there, Katherine; what is written there?

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Three big Noes; what does that mean

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: I will tell you that too, later on. There, Petra; tell sooty-face to run over to the "Badger's" with that, as quick as she can. Hurry up!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Well, I think I have had a visit from every one of the devil's messengers to-day! But now I am going to sharpen my pen till they can feel its point; I shall dip it in venom and gall; I shall hurl my inkpot at their heads!

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, but we are going away, you know, Thomas.

session_5.speaker: STAGE
session_5.text: (PETRA comes back.)

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Well?

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: She has gone with it.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Good.—Going away, did you say? No, I'll be hanged if we are going away! We are going to stay where we are, Katherine!

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Stay here?

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Here, in the town?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, here. This is the field of battle—this is where the fight will be. This is where I shall triumph! As soon as I have had my trousers sewn up I shall go out and look for another house. We must have a roof over our heads for the winter.

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: That you shall have in my house.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Can I?

session_5.speaker: Horsier
session_5.text: Yes, quite well. I have plenty of room, and I am almost never at home.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: How good of you, Captain Horster!

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Thank you!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Thank you, thank you! That is one trouble over! Now I can set to work in earnest at once. There is an endless amount of things to look through here, Katherine! Luckily I shall have all my time at my disposal; because I have been dismissed from the Baths, you know.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Oh yes, I expected that.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: And they want to take my practice away from me too. Let them! I have got the poor people to fall back upon, anyway—those that don't pay anything; and, after all, they need me most, too. But, by Jove, they will have to listen to me; I shall preach to them in season and out of season, as it says somewhere.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: But, dear Thomas, I should have thought events had showed you what use it is to preach.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: You are really ridiculous, Katherine. Do you want me to let myself be beaten off the field by public opinion and the compact majority and all that devilry? No, thank you! And what I want to do is so simple and clear and straightforward. I only want to drum into the heads of these curs the fact that the liberals are the most insidious enemies of freedom—that party programmes strangle every young and vigorous truth—that considerations of expediency turn morality and justice upside down—and that they will end by making life here unbearable. Don't you think, Captain Horster, that I ought to be able to make people understand that?

session_5.speaker: Horster
session_5.text: Very likely; I don't know much about such things myself.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Well, look here—I will explain! It is the party leaders that must be exterminated. A party leader is like a wolf, you see—like a voracious wolf. He requires a certain number of smaller victims to prey upon every year, if he is to live. Just look at Hovstad and Aslaksen! How many smaller victims have they not put an end to—or at any rate maimed and mangled until they are fit for nothing except to be householders or subscribers to the "People's Messenger"! Come here, Katherine—look how beautifully the sun shines to-day! And this lovely spring air I am drinking in!

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, if only we could live on sunshine and spring air,

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Oh, you will have to pinch and save a bit—then we shall get along. That gives me very little concern. What is much worse is, that I know of no one who is liberal-minded and high-minded enough to venture to take up my work after me.

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Don't think about that, father; you have plenty of time before you.—Hello, here are the boys already!

session_5.speaker: STAGE
session_5.text: (EJLIF and MORTEN come in from the sitting-room.)

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Have you got a holiday?

session_5.speaker: Morten
session_5.text: No; but we were fighting with the other boys between lessons—

session_5.speaker: Ejlif
session_5.text: That isn't true; it was the other boys were fighting with us.

session_5.speaker: Morten
session_5.text: Well, and then Mr. Rorlund said we had better stay at home for a day or two.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: I have it! I have it, by Jove! You shall never set foot in the school again!

session_5.speaker: The Boys
session_5.text: No more school!

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: But, Thomas—

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Never, I say. I will educate you myself; that is to say, you shan't learn a blessed thing—

session_5.speaker: Morten
session_5.text: Hooray!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: but I will make liberal-minded and high-minded men of you. You must help me with that, Petra.

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Yes, father, you may be sure I will.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: And my school shall be in the room where they insulted me and called me an enemy of the people. But we are too few as we are; I must have at least twelve boys to begin with.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: You will certainly never get them in this town.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: We shall. Don't you know any street urchins—regular ragamuffins—?

session_5.speaker: Morten
session_5.text: Yes, father, I know lots!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: That's capital! Bring me some specimens of them. I am going to experiment with curs, just for once; there may be some exceptional heads among them.

session_5.speaker: Morten
session_5.text: And what are we going to do, when you have made liberal-minded and high-minded men of us?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Then you shall drive all the wolves out of the country, my boys!

session_5.speaker: STAGE
session_5.text: (EJLIF looks rather doubtful about it; MORTEN jumps about crying "Hurrah!")

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Let us hope it won't be the wolves that will drive you out of the country, Thomas.

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Are you out of your mind, Katherine? Drive me out! Now—when I am the strongest man in the town!

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: The strongest—now?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes, and I will go so far as to say that now I am the strongest man in the whole world.

session_5.speaker: Morten
session_5.text: I say!

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Hush! You mustn't say anything about it yet; but I have made a great discovery.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Another one?

session_5.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_5.text: Yes. It is this, let me tell you—that the strongest man in the world is he who stands most alone.

session_5.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_5.text: Oh, Thomas, Thomas!

session_5.speaker: Petra
session_5.text: Father!
